does: This paper introduces Anchored Direct Preference Optimization (ADPO), a policy alignment method that reformulates reinforcement learning from human feedback through anchored logits, which operate as differential log-ratio coordinates rather than absolute probabilities. The core contribution derives from first-principles KL-regularized RL, showing that optimal policies should decouple response quality from prior popularity. ADPO parameterizes this structure explicitly, creating an implicit trust region via curvature scaling. The method unifies supervised fine-tuning, reinforcement learning, and ranking objectives geometrically. Theoretically, it addresses probability smearing in SFT while avoiding mode-seeking instability of reverse-KL approaches. Empirically, a listwise ranking variant of ADPO achieves state-of-the-art results on reasoning tasks, demonstrating both improved alignment and training stability.
connects: ADPO builds directly on recent work in preference-based policy alignment, extending direct preference optimization (DPO) and similar methods that avoid explicit reward modeling. It responds to known limitations in existing approaches: the probability mass diffusion problem affecting standard supervised fine-tuning, and the distribution mismatch issues in classical RLHF. The work connects to foundational literature on KL-regularized RL and inverse reinforcement learning, translating theoretical insights into practical algorithmic form. It also relates to broader research on understanding why reference policies matter in alignment and how to better leverage human preference data. The geometric perspective linking SFT, RL, and ranking objectives draws on recent work reconceptualizing policy optimization through manifold geometry and differential coordinates.
enables: This framework opens several research directions in policy alignment. The anchored logit parameterization could inspire new trust region mechanisms for language model training beyond standard KL penalties, potentially improving sample efficiency and stability of alignment procedures. The geometric unification suggests deeper connections between supervised and reinforcement learning approaches that may yield hybrid training schemes. The decoupling of quality from popularity could enable better handling of imbalanced preference datasets and long-tailed instruction distributions. Future work might extend ADPO to multi-objective alignment, offline RL settings with incomplete preferences, or application to vision and multimodal models. The theoretical framework also provides tools for analyzing other alignment methods through the lens of differential coordinates, potentially informing design of future preference optimization algorithms.
